responses.content: t first, little Tuthmosis III was considered the pharaoh, with Hatshepsut just his second-in-command. But a small child could not be an effective ruler. As Hatshepsut settled into her role as regent, she gradually took on more and more of the royal decision-making. She appointed officials and advisors; dealt with the priests; appeared in public ceremonies first behind, then beside, and eventually in front of her nephew. Gradually, over seven years, her power and influence grew. In the end, Hatshepsut was ruling Egypt in all but name.

questions.question: Highlight a detail from Excerpt 2 that shows Elena taking action to empower herself.

responses.content: Hatshepsut, royal daughter of Pharaoh Tuthmosis and his Great Wife Ahmose, grew up in an Egypt that was peaceful, prosperous, and respected throughout the known world.
responses.bucket_rationale: The student's highlight is: 'Hatshepsut, royal daughter of Pharaoh Tuthmosis and his Great Wife Ahmose, grew up in an Egypt that was peaceful, prosperous, and respected throughout the known world.' This does not show an obstacle or challenge Hatshepsut faced as a woman in power. It only describes her background and the state of Egypt, not the difficulties she encountered due to her gender or her role. Therefore, the response is incorrect.

responses.content: ) There was no word in the language of ancient Egypt for a female ruler; a queen was simply the wife of a king. Hatshepsut had no choice: she had to call herself pharaoh, or king—a male title. She was concerned with preserving and continuing traditional order as much as possible, so to the people of Egypt she made herself look like a man in her role as pharaoh. In ceremonies, she wore a man’s short kilt instead of a woman’s long dress, much as she had as a child. Around her neck she wore a king’s broad collar. She even fastened a false golden beard to her chin. When she wrote about herself as pharaoh, sometimes she referred to herself as he, other times as she. This would be very confusing for historians trying to uncover her identity thousands of years later.
responses.bucket_rationale: The student's most recent highlight is: 'There was no word in the language of ancient Egypt for a female ruler; a queen was simply the wife of a king. Hatshepsut had no choice: she had to call herself pharaoh, or king—a male title. She was concerned with preserving and continuing traditional order as much as possible, so to the people of Egypt she made herself look like a man in her role as pharaoh. In ceremonies, she wore a man’s short kilt instead of a woman’s long dress, much as she had as a child. Around her neck she wore a king’s broad collar. She even fastened a false golden beard to her chin. When she wrote about herself as pharaoh, sometimes she referred to herself as he, other times as she. This would be very confusing for historians trying to uncover her identity thousands of years later.' responses.content:
There is no reliable record of exactly when or how it happened, but at some point, Hatshepsut took a bold and unprecedented step: She had herself crowned pharaoh with the large, heavy, red-and-white double crown of the two Egypts, north and south. Since all pharaohs took a throne name, a sort of symbolic name, upon their coronation, Hatshepsut chose Maatkare (maht-KAH-ray). Maat, that crucial cosmic order, was important to Hatshepsut. Egypt required a strong pharaoh to ensure maat. Hatshepsut could be that pharaoh—even if she did happen to be a woman.

(13) A few women had tried to rule Egypt before, but never with such a valid claim to the throne or at such a time of peace and prosperity. When Queens Nitocris and Sobekneferu had come to the throne in earlier dynasties, Egypt had been suffering from political problems, and there had been no male heirs. These women had not ruled long or well, and neither had had the audacity to proclaim herself pharaoh. Hatshepsut would be different.
responses.bucket_rationale: The student's most recent highlight is from the Hatshepsut excerpt, not from Excerpt 2: Elena, as the question required. The question specifically asked for a detail from Excerpt 2 that shows Elena taking action to empower herself. The highlighted text is not from the correct section and does not answer the question. Therefore, the response is incorrect.

questions.question: What happened during Donna's first visit to a volcano that made her want to continue studying them?

responses.content: O’Meara grew up in the New England countryside. There are no volcanoes in Connecticut, but in the spring and summer there were fierce thunder and lightning storms that thrilled Donna. In school, her favorite classes were earth science and biology. However, instead of turning her love for science into a career after graduation, she became an artist, photographer, and writer. As she worked on different magazines and books, she gradually began to realize that something was missing in her life?
responses.bucket_rationale: The response includes evidence from paragraph 4, mentioning that Donna was thrilled by fierce thunder and lightning storms and that her favorite classes were earth science and biology. These details directly show Donna's early interest in natural forces, meeting both the form and accuracy requirements for the 'correct' bucket.

questions.question: Reread paragraph 8. Based on the context in the paragraph, what does the word “document” mean?

responses.content: She got hit by a ball of volcano
responses.bucket_rationale: The response references being hit by a 'ball of volcano,' which is not accurate to the text. The text describes Donna dodging lava bombs and feeling heat from underground lava melting her shoes, but does not mention her being hit by anything. Additionally, the response does not connect the experience to her motivation for continuing to study volcanoes. This is a fabrication and a misreading of the key scene, so it is classified as incorrect.

responses.content: In 1986, Donna visited her first volcano as Steve’s research assistant. After dodging lava bombs and feeling the heat from underground lava melting her shoes, Donna was hooked. The following year, she and Steve were married on lava that had oozed from Kilauea on Hawaii and hardened. Lava that hardens creates new landforms, and some volcanoes, such as Surtsey off the coast of Iceland, actually create new islands?
responses.bucket_rationale: The response includes key details from the text about Donna's first volcano visit: she was Steve's research assistant, dodged lava bombs, felt heat from underground lava melting her shoes, and became 'hooked.' These details match the source text and explain why she wanted to continue studying volcanoes. The answer is accurate and complete.

questions.question: Why is it significant that Donna lives on top of Kilauea volcano?

responses.content: From their home, Donna and Steve run Volcano Watch International (VWI). The O’Mearas’ organization is dedicated to understanding how Earth’s active volcanoes work. VWI uses photos and video to educate people about the dangers of volcanoes. Their mission is to travel to active volcanoes and document the eruptions. The first volcano Donna studied was Kilauea, which is a shield volcano?
responses.bucket_rationale: The response repeats the text and shows engagement, but it does not provide a definition or meaning for the word 'document.' It does not mention recording, capturing, or creating records, which is necessary for a 'correct' classification. However, it is not incorrect because it does not provide a wrong meaning or show limited effort. It fits the 'partial-understanding' bucket as it attempts to engage with the word in context but misses the key aspect of its meaning.

responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'They study the volcano' shows the learner is engaging with the text and attempting to define 'document,' but misses the key aspect of recording or capturing information (photos, videos, written records). questions.question: How does Malala know the school is closing permanently?

responses.content: Before I closed the school door I looked
responses.bucket_rationale: The response references a specific moment from the text ('Before I closed the school door I looked'), showing text-specific engagement. However, it does not mention the key detail that no date was announced for the start of the next term, which is how Malala knows the school is closing permanently. Therefore, this fits the 'partial-understanding' bucket.

responses.content: We had a special assembly that final morning, but it was hard to hear with the noise of helicopters overhead. Some of us spoke out against what was happening in our valley. The bell rang for the very last time, and then Madam Maryam announced it was winter vacation. But unlike in other years no date was announced for the start of next term. Even so, some teachers still gave us homework. In the yard I hugged all my friends. I looked at the honors board and wondered if my name would ever appear on it again. Exams were due in March, but how could they take place? Coming first didn’t matter if you couldn’t study at all. When someone takes away your pens you realize quite how important education is?
responses.bucket_rationale: The response includes text-specific details such as the special assembly, Madam Maryam's announcement, and Malala's emotional reaction. However, it does not specifically mention the key detail that no date was announced for the start of the next term, which is the main clue Malala had that the school might be closing for good. The response demonstrates engagement with the text but misses the specific threshold for 'correct.'

responses.content: The government
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'The government' is text-specific but incomplete. It does not mention the key detail about no date being announced for the next term, which is required for a 'correct' classification. However, it is not generic, off-topic, or contradictory, and it shows some engagement with the text, so it fits 'partial-understanding.'

questions.question: Based on the text, why does the Taliban condemn the education of girls and not the education of boys?

responses.content: When I got home, I cried and cried. I didn’t want to stop learning. I was only eleven years old, but I felt as though I had lost everything. I had told everyone in my class that the Taliban wouldn’t go through with it. “They’re just like our politicians—they talk the talk, but they won’t do anything,” I’d said. But then they went ahead and closed our school and I felt embarrassed. I couldn’t control myself. I was crying, my mother was crying, but my father insisted, “You will go to school!
responses.bucket_rationale: The response shows understanding of the emotional impact of the school closing and references the Taliban's actions, but it does not explain the Taliban's reasoning (fear of Westernization) or note the inconsistency that boys' schools were allowed to reopen while girls' schools were not. The answer is text-specific and demonstrates engagement, but it is missing the key reasoning or acknowledgment of the double standard required for a 'correct' classification.

responses.content: sick?” The Taliban is against education because they think that when a child reads a book or learns English or studies science he or she will become Westernized?
responses.bucket_rationale: The response correctly identifies that the Taliban is against education because they think it will make children Westernized, which is a key element from the text. However, it does not mention the inconsistency or hypocrisy that boys' schools are allowed to reopen while girls' schools are closed, nor does it note the double standard. Therefore, it fits the 'partial-understanding' bucket, as it has one key element (reasoning) but not both (reasoning and inconsistency).

responses.content: Once Muslim Khan had said girls should not go to school and learn Western ways. This from a man who had lived so long in America! He insisted he would have his own education system. “What would Muslim Khan use instead of the stethoscope and the thermometer?” my father asked. “Are there any Eastern instruments that will treat the sick?” The Taliban is against education because they think that when a child reads a book or learns English or studies science he or she will become Westernized.

questions.question: Brainstorm 2-3 impacts the media had on Malala. You can write a list, use complete sentences, or use whatever format helps you think best.

responses.content: One day we got a call from America, from a student at Stanford University. Her name was Shiza Shahid and she came from Islamabad. She had seen the New York Times documentary Class Dismissed in Swat Valley and tracked us down. We saw then the power of the media and she became a great support to us. My father was almost bursting with pride at how I came across in the documentary. “Look at her,” he told Adam Ellick. “Don’t you think she is meant for the skies?” Fathers can be very embarrassing.
responses.bucket_rationale: The student's highlight is much longer than the acceptable character limit for this question. questions.question: Which statement best describes a central idea of the text?

questions.question: Which excerpt from the text most strongly supports the answer to the previous question?

responses.content: C. “Why do leaders accept that for children in developing countries, only basic literacy is sufficient, when their own children do homework in Algebra, Mathematics, Science and Physics?”
